The patient is positioned on the operating table in a leg split position, which facilitates access to the surgical site for the cholecystectomy procedure.

And the port position is, as mentioned in this particular figure, was umbilical trocar, which is a twelve millimeter.
The surgeon inserts a second 12mm trocar, followed by two additional 5mm trocars, into the abdominal cavity for the cholecystectomy procedure.
Following the establishment of pneumoperitoneum and placement of trocars, the surgeon attempts to grasp the fundus of the gallbladder, encountering dense adhesions of the omentum.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to address dense adhesions at the fundic region of the gallbladder and the edges of the liver. The gallbladder appears distended.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect and remove the dense adhesions surrounding the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully detach the attachments near the neck of the gallbladder, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon observes the duodenum adherent to the distended and dilated common bile duct (CVD), indicating dense adhesions between these structures.
The surgeon uses an Argonics device to cut and remove adhesions over the common bile duct (CBD) at the gallbladder, ensuring clear separation of these structures.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the fat over the common bile duct (CBD), fully exposing the CBD.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the gallbladder from the common bile duct (CBD), carefully dissecting the adhesions between these structures.
The surgeon uses a hook to mark the incision over the well-exposed common bile duct (CBD), preparing for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon plans a two-centimeter incision over the common bile duct (CBD) using a hook with monopolar current, preparing to make the incision in the exposed area.

The surgeon opens the common bile duct (CBD), resulting in a visible gush of bile from the incision site. A suction device is used to remove the bile, and the incision is extended craniocaudally using monopolar current.
Due to the presence of bile, the surgeon encounters difficulty in extending the incision over the common bile duct (CBD).
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to extend the incision on the common bile duct craniocaudally, following the initial marking and separation of the gallbladder from the common bile duct.
The surgeon extends the incision towards the hilum and porta hepatis using a surgical instrument, following the previously marked path on the common bile duct.
Around two centimeter after taking incident.
A large stone is delivered from the incision in the common bile duct (CBD), followed by additional stones that are easily extracted with the aid of suction and manual pressure on the CBD.
A second stone is visibly emerging from the incision site, facilitated by gentle pressure and suction.
The surgeon identifies a stone in the common hepatic duct and successfully delivers it out, marking the removal of three stones in total. A plastic bag is placed in the abdomen to collect the stones.
The surgeon places multiple stones into a plastic bag within the abdominal cavity to prevent misplacement and facilitate retrieval.
The surgeon inspects the common hepatic duct, revealing a stent and a small stone. The stone is carefully removed using an appropriate surgical instrument.
The surgeon uses forceps to manipulate and remove stones from the common hepatic duct, placing them into a plastic bag within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon has nearly cleared the common hepatic duct, with visible progress in removing stones from the duct.
The surgical field shows the common hepatic duct cleared of stones, with no remaining stones visible.
The surgeon removes four stones from the common bile duct (CBD) and uses suction in the left hand to irrigate the common hepatic duct, ensuring clear visualization of the ductal structures.
The surgeon uses irrigation to flush the common hepatic duct, aiming to dislodge any remaining small stones visible within the duct.
The surgeon initiates irrigation of the common hepatic duct, using a fluid stream to clear any remaining debris or stones.
The surgeon inspects the common hepatic duct, confirming visually that there are no remaining stones present.
The surgeon observes a stent visibly positioned within the left hepatic duct.
The surgeon attempts to clear the lower common bile duct (CBD), successfully extracting a large stone from the duct.
Two stones are visible as they emerge from the common bile duct during the irrigation process.
The surgeon uses an intestinal grasp to apply pressure from the duodenal aspect, attempting to manipulate or move structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a suction tip to attempt to grab and push stones from the lower common bile duct (CBD) into the incision site. Despite efforts, one stone remains impacted in the lower CBD.

The surgical team attempts to remove a stone from the lower common bile duct (CBD) using straight sticks, but finds it impossible. They decide to insert a flexible pediatric gastroscope, measuring six millimeters in diameter, into the CBD for further visualization and manipulation.

The video shows a large stone impacted at the lower end of the common bile duct (CBD), with a stent visible adjacent to the stone. The tight space prevents the insertion of a catheter or balloon detection catheter.
passing through or it was not possible to pass just beside it.

An adult gastroscope, approximately 9.3 mm in diameter, is inserted to visualize the impacted stone in the lower common bile duct (CBD). The surgeon uses foreign body grasping forceps to attempt dislodging the stone from its position.
But still it was not possible.

Under gastroscope guidance, a balloon extraction catheter measuring 4.5 mm French is inserted into the lower common bile duct (CBD) below the impacted stone. The balloon is then inflated to 15 mm, visibly expanding within the duct.

The balloon catheter is inflated to 15 mm, successfully dislodging the large impacted stone from the lower end of the common bile duct. The stone is then pushed from the outside, allowing for its removal.

The previously made incision reveals the impacted stone being extracted. The lower common bile duct (CBD) is cleared, and a pediatric gastroscope is inserted into the lower CBD, followed by saline irrigation.
The surgical team administers a saline wash to the lower common bile duct (CBD) to assess the clearance of any remaining stones or debris.
The surgeon observes the stent in the lower CBD, noting that complete clearance of the duct has been achieved alongside the stent.
The video shows the clear visualization of the lower common bile duct (CBD) after the removal of the impacted stone.
The surgeon confirms complete clearance of the lower common bile duct (CVD), with no visible obstructions remaining.
The surgeon uses a pediatric gastroscope to examine the common hepatic duct, following the clearance of the lower common bile duct.
The surgeon visualizes the right and left hepatic ducts, performing a saline wash to confirm complete clearance of the ducts.
The surgical field shows the common bile duct (CBD) with complete clearance achieved, visible alongside the stent.
The surgeon uses a pediatric gastroscope to visually confirm the complete clearance of the lower common bile duct (CBD).
The surgeon closes the incision in the common bile duct (CBD) using a continuous suture technique with 2-0 Maxon suture material.
The surgeon observes the stent positioned in situ within the biliary system.
The stent is clearly visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the closure of the common bile duct incision using continuous sutures, ensuring the incision is fully sealed.
The surgical team achieves a complete watertight closure of the common bile duct incision using continuous sutures.
After achieving hemostasis and closure of the common bile duct (CBD), the surgeon proceeds to remove the gallbladder.
The surgeon begins dissection of the hepatocystic triangle, focusing on separating the anatomical structures within this area.
The surgeon begins dissection to locate the cystic duct and cystic artery, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon exposes the peritoneum surrounding the gallbladder, revealing its position both laterally and medially within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon observes a notably large cystic duct during the cholecystectomy procedure, highlighting its size in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the cystic duct, noting its large diameter during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon observes the opening of the large cystic duct into the lower common bile duct (CBD) on the lateral aspect during the dissection of the hepatocystic triangle.
The surgeon performs dissection of the hepatocystic triangle using a hook, followed by Maryland forceps, carefully manipulating the tissue to expose the cystic duct and artery.
The surgeon achieves simultaneous hemostasis in the surgical field, ensuring control of bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a Maryland forceps to dissect the cystic artery, which is visible below a lymph node in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the separation of the cystic duct, which is visibly large in diameter, from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, ensuring clear visualization of the cystic duct and artery, as well as the hepatocystic triangle, confirming proper anatomical identification before proceeding with further dissection.
The surgeon uses a hook to carefully remove the remaining adhesions in the hepatocystic triangle, ensuring clear separation of the gallbladder from surrounding tissues.
The hepatic cystic triangle is fully dissected, revealing the cystic duct, cystic artery, gallbladder, and gallbladder fossa clearly visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon clears the cystic duct from all surrounding tissues, ensuring it is fully exposed and isolated within the surgical field.
The surgeon clears adhesive tissue from the area surrounding the cystic duct, ensuring a clean surgical field.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps to coagulate the cystic artery, then places a clip over it before cutting the artery.
The surgeon clears the cystic duct from surrounding tissue, ensuring it is fully exposed within the dissected hepatic cystic triangle.
Hemoclips are applied to the cystic duct, securing it before the duct is cut, completing the dissection within the hepatic cystic triangle.
The surgeon separates the gallbladder from the gallbladder fossa, detaching it from its anatomical position.
The surgeon achieves simultaneous hemostasis, ensuring control of bleeding in the surgical field after clearing adhesive tissue and securing the cystic duct and artery.
The surgeon places the separated gallbladder, containing stones, into a retrieval bag and removes it through the umbilical port.
The video shows the removed gallbladder alongside eight stones extracted from the common bile duct (CBD), displayed for visual inspection.
